name: Springbank 10 Year
description: Springbank 10 is the flagship of Scotland's most stubbornly traditional distillery and the standard-bearer of the near-vanished Campbeltown style: oily, briny, lightly smoky and faintly industrial in the most flattering sense — drinkers call the signature note 'Campbeltown funk'. Every stage of production, from floor-malting the barley to bottling, happens on one site, essentially by hand, which caps output and has turned the 10 into one of whisky's most chased allocated bottles; street prices now run roughly double the official price. Bottled at 46% ABV without chill filtration or colouring, it layers salted citrus, vanilla cream, damp earth and wisps of smoke over a distinctly chewy texture. For many enthusiasts it is the benchmark of characterful, old-school Scotch made with no concessions to scale.
history: Springbank was founded in 1828 on the site of an illicit still, and the Mitchell family — connected with the distillery from its early years — still owns it through J & A Mitchell & Co, making it the oldest independent family-owned distillery in Scotland. Campbeltown once hosted over thirty distilleries and styled itself the whisky capital of the world before crashing in the 1920s; Springbank was one of the very few survivors. The family also revived neighbouring Glengyle in 2004, helping preserve Campbeltown's status as a recognised whisky region.
production: Springbank is the only Scottish distillery to malt 100% of its barley on its own floors, kilning it briefly over peat (roughly six hours of smoke) for a lightly peated profile. The spirit is distilled two and a half times — a partial triple distillation using a direct-fired wash still — then matured in roughly 60% ex-bourbon and 40% ex-sherry casks. Bottling, on site, is at 46% ABV, non-chill filtered and without added colour.

tastingNotes.nose: Brine, wet wool, light peat smoke, lemon and vanilla custard with an earthy, oily undertow.
tastingNotes.palate: Chewy and coastal — salted caramel, orchard fruit, malt, black pepper and that savoury Campbeltown funk.
tastingNotes.finish: Long, gently smoky and maritime, drying to minerals and sea salt.

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature (18–20 °C); no water needed at 46%
facts: The only distillery in Scotland that performs 100% of production — floor malting, distilling, maturing and bottling — on a single site.
Its spirit is distilled two and a half times, a hybrid of double and triple distillation.
Springbank makes three distinct whiskies on the same stills: lightly peated Springbank, heavily peated Longrow and unpeated triple-distilled Hazelburn.
Campbeltown had more than thirty distilleries in the Victorian era; today it has three, two of them owned by Springbank's parent family.

timeline.event: Springbank founded on the site of a former illicit still
Owners J & A Mitchell revive the neighbouring Glengyle distillery, safeguarding Campbeltown as a whisky region
Surging demand pushes the core range onto strict allocation worldwide

name: Johnnie Walker Black Label 12 Year
description: Johnnie Walker Black Label is the world's benchmark deluxe blended Scotch — a 12-year-old marriage of several dozen malt and grain whiskies from across Scotland, built around a thread of Islay smoke that gives it more character than most blends at the price. Vanilla, raisin and toffee sweetness from the grain whiskies carries dried orchard fruit, winter spice and that signature wisp of peat. Sold in the brand's square bottle with the label set at its trademark slant, it is stocked in practically every bar on earth and mixes as well as it sips; bartenders reach for it in highballs and Scotch cocktails as a smoky-but-safe default. As a one-bottle summary of what Scotland's regions taste like blended together, nothing else is so widely agreed upon.
history: John Walker began selling whisky from his Kilmarnock grocery in 1820, but it was his son Alexander who scaled the business, introducing the square bottle in 1860 and the blend that became Black Label — originally Extra Special Old Highland Whisky. The colour-coded name was formalised in 1909, a year after the Striding Man figure was sketched by cartoonist Tom Browne. Johnnie Walker grew into the best-selling Scotch whisky on the planet, now shipping well over twenty million cases a year for owner Diageo.
production: Black Label is blended from several dozen single malt and grain whiskies, each matured at least twelve years; the exact recipe is undisclosed and adjusted cask by cask to hold the flavour constant. Malts from Speyside and the Highlands supply fruit and depth while Islay and coastal malts contribute the smoky backbone, all rounded on a base of soft grain whisky. The components are married, chill filtered and bottled at 40% ABV.
barrel: Component whiskies matured a minimum of 12 years in ex-bourbon and ex-sherry oak casks
coloring: Caramel colour (E150a) used for batch consistency

tastingNotes.nose: Toffee, vanilla, orange zest and dried fruit with a curl of distant peat smoke.
tastingNotes.palate: Smooth and rounded — raisins, butterscotch, sweet malt and baking spice over gentle smoke.
tastingNotes.finish: Medium, warming and lightly smoky with lingering caramel and spice.

servingTemp: Neat, over ice, or in a 1:3 highball with chilled soda
facts: Alexander Walker adopted the square bottle in 1860 so more bottles survived sea freight and fit in a case.
The label is printed at a precise 24-degree slant, a branding quirk maintained since the nineteenth century.
The Striding Man logo was drawn in 1908 by cartoonist Tom Browne, reputedly on the back of a menu.
Johnnie Walker is the world's best-selling Scotch whisky, sold in roughly 180 countries.

timeline.event: John Walker starts selling whisky from his Kilmarnock grocery
Square bottle introduced by Alexander Walker
Tom Browne sketches the Striding Man emblem
The 12-year-old blend is renamed Black Label

name: Jameson Irish Whiskey
description: Jameson is Irish whiskey to most of the world — a triple-distilled blend of pot still and grain whiskeys that outsells every other Irish brand many times over, moving more than ten million cases a year. Its profile is deliberately friendly: light and smooth with grassy malt, vanilla, honey and a gentle nutty spice from the pot still component, with no smoke anywhere. That easy character made it the engine of Irish whiskey's global revival and a fixture of shot-and-beer culture, Irish coffees and whiskey sours alike. Serious drinkers graduate to its pot-still siblings, but Jameson's role in the category is hard to overstate: it kept Irish whiskey alive through the industry's collapse and remains the door through which most drinkers enter it.
history: The Bow Street distillery in Dublin was founded in 1780, and John Jameson — a Scot from Alloa — took control around the turn of that century, building it into one of Dublin's great whiskey houses. After Irish whiskey's twentieth-century collapse, Jameson's owners merged with rivals Powers and Cork Distilleries in 1966 to form Irish Distillers, and production moved to the New Midleton distillery in County Cork in 1975. Under Pernod Ricard ownership since 1988, Jameson has grown from under half a million cases to more than ten million, surpassed in 2022.
production: Jameson blends triple-distilled single pot still whiskey — made from a mash of malted and unmalted barley in Midleton's enormous copper pots — with lighter grain whiskey distilled from maize in column stills. The proportions are undisclosed. Both components mature in ex-bourbon barrels and ex-sherry butts, legally at least three years though typically longer, before being married and bottled at 40% ABV.
barrel: Ex-bourbon American oak barrels and ex-Oloroso sherry butts

tastingNotes.nose: Light floral malt, cut grass, vanilla and a touch of sherried orchard fruit.
tastingNotes.palate: Smooth and sweet — honey, vanilla, toasted wood and a soft pot-still nuttiness.
tastingNotes.finish: Short and clean with mild spice and lingering honeyed malt.

servingTemp: Neat, over ice, or with ginger ale and lime
facts: John Jameson was a Scot from Alloa who married into the Haig whisky dynasty before building his Dublin whiskey empire.
Every label carries the Jameson family motto 'Sine Metu' — without fear — said by family lore to honour ancestors who fought pirates.
Jameson outsells the next-largest Irish whiskey brand several times over, passing ten million annual cases in 2022.
Production left Dublin in 1975; the original Bow Street site is now a visitor centre in the Smithfield district.

timeline.event: Bow Street distillery founded in Dublin; John Jameson soon takes the helm
Jameson joins Powers and Cork Distilleries to form Irish Distillers
Production moves to the New Midleton distillery in County Cork
Annual sales surpass ten million cases

name: Redbreast 12 Year
description: Redbreast 12 is the benchmark of single pot still Irish whiskey — the style, unique to Ireland, made from a mixed mash of malted and unmalted barley distilled in copper pots. The raw barley delivers the category's signatures: a creamy, almost oily texture and a bristling spice the Irish call pot-still character, layered here with oloroso sherry fruit, baked apple, hazelnut and Christmas-cake richness. When Irish whiskey nearly died in the twentieth century, Redbreast came closer to extinction than any famous name, disappearing in 1985 before a 1991 revival that grew into today's acclaimed range. Critics routinely place the 12 among the best-value whiskeys of any nation, and it is the bottle most often used to show doubters that Irish whiskey can be every bit as serious as Scotch.
history: Redbreast began as a bonder's whiskey: from 1912 the London wine and spirit merchant W & A Gilbey sold Jameson-distilled pot still whiskey matured in its own casks under the name, inspired — the story goes — by the chairman's fondness for birds, hence the robin. As Irish whiskey contracted, bonding died out and Redbreast was discontinued in 1985. Irish Distillers revived it at Midleton in 1991, and the brand has since grown into the most decorated family in the single pot still revival.
production: A mash of roughly equal malted and unmalted barley — the exact ratio is undisclosed — is triple distilled in Midleton's large copper pot stills, taking a heavy, flavour-dense cut. The spirit matures a minimum of twelve years in a combination of first-fill and refill ex-bourbon barrels and first-fill oloroso sherry butts from Jerez, then is vatted and bottled at 40% ABV. The unmalted barley is what gives the whiskey its distinctive creamy weight and peppery spice.
barrel: First-fill and refill ex-bourbon barrels with first-fill Oloroso sherry butts

tastingNotes.nose: Baked apple, sherried dried fruit, toasted nuts, marzipan and a cut of fresh pot-still spice.
tastingNotes.palate: Creamy and full — Christmas cake, raisins, hazelnut, ginger and clove riding a silky, oily texture.
tastingNotes.finish: Long and spicy, sweet fruit slowly drying to oak and barley.

facts: Named for the robin redbreast, reputedly a nod to the birdwatching hobby of a Gilbey's chairman.
One of the last surviving 'bonder's whiskeys' — merchants once bought new spirit from distilleries and matured it in their own casks.
Discontinued in 1985 at the nadir of Irish whiskey, and revived by Irish Distillers in 1991.
Single pot still whiskey's mixed mash of malted and unmalted barley originated partly as a dodge around a British tax on malted barley.

timeline.event: W & A Gilbey first sells Redbreast as a bonded Jameson pot still whiskey
Redbreast discontinued as bonding dies out
Irish Distillers relaunches Redbreast 12 from Midleton

name: Green Spot
description: Green Spot is a living relic of Ireland's whiskey-bonding tradition — a single pot still whiskey created for the Dublin wine merchants Mitchell & Son, who once daubed coloured spots of paint on their maturing casks to mark intended ages. Green marked ten years; blue, yellow and red marked others. For decades it was one of the rarest famous whiskeys in the world, a few hundred cases a year sold almost entirely over one Dublin shop counter, and the only bonder's whiskey to survive the industry's collapse. Now produced at Midleton with wider distribution, it is fresher and orchard-brighter than its sibling Redbreast: green apple, gooseberry and menthol over honeyed malt and classic pot-still spice. Its survival story and singular style have made it a quiet cult favourite among Irish whiskey drinkers.
history: Mitchell & Son, wine and provisions merchants in Dublin since 1805, began bonding whiskey in 1887, sending their own casks to the Jameson distillery to be filled and maturing them in cellars beneath the city. The Spot range emerged in the early twentieth century, and Green Spot alone survived Irish whiskey's long decline — by the 1980s reportedly down to around five hundred cases a year. Irish Distillers now makes it at Midleton in partnership with the Mitchell family; a US launch followed in 2014, and the discontinued Yellow and Red Spots have since been revived.
production: Green Spot is triple distilled at Midleton from a mixed mash of malted and unmalted barley — proportions undisclosed — in the single pot still tradition. It is vatted from whiskeys aged roughly seven to ten years, matured in ex-bourbon American oak with a portion (about a quarter) drawn from sherry casks, and bottled at 40% ABV. The unmalted barley lends the trademark green-orchard snap and peppery spice.
barrel: Ex-bourbon American oak barrels with roughly a quarter ex-sherry casks

tastingNotes.nose: Green apple, pear, cut hay and menthol with honey and toasted oak.
tastingNotes.palate: Lively orchard fruit and barley sugar, cream, clove and peppery pot-still spice.
tastingNotes.finish: Medium, crisp and gently spiced, fading through green fruit to vanilla.

facts: The 'spot' names come from Mitchell & Son literally daubing paint spots on casks — blue, green, yellow and red for different intended ages.
For much of the late twentieth century Green Spot was the only surviving bonder's Irish whiskey, sold almost exclusively at one Dublin shop.
Mitchell & Son, founded in 1805, still trades in Dublin and remains involved with the brand.
Green Spot reached the United States for the first time in 2014.

timeline.event: Mitchell & Son founded in Dublin
Mitchells begin bonding whiskey filled at Jameson's Bow Street distillery
Green Spot launched in the United States

name: Bushmills 10 Year Single Malt
description: Bushmills 10 is the flagship single malt of Ireland's north and a gentle counterpoint to Scotch malt orthodoxy: made from 100% malted barley like its Scottish cousins, but unpeated and triple distilled in small pots, which strips out smoke and weight and leaves honey, vanilla, ripe orchard fruit and milk chocolate in a light, silky frame. It comes from the distillery in the County Antrim village of Bushmills that anchors one of whiskey's most repeated claims — a 1608 royal licence to distil in the surrounding territory, which the brand wears as 'the oldest licensed whiskey distillery in the world'. As an approachable, well-priced introduction to malt whiskey without smoke, it has few rivals, and it anchors a range whose older sherried siblings enjoy strong critical regard.
history: In 1608 King James I granted Sir Thomas Phillips a licence to distil in the territory around Bushmills — the basis of the brand's 'oldest licensed' claim — though the Old Bushmills Distillery Company itself was registered in 1784. A fire levelled the distillery in 1885, and rebuilt Victorian buildings still stand. Uniquely, Bushmills kept faith with malt whiskey through the era when Dublin's pot still houses dominated. Ownership has churned in the modern era: Irish Distillers from 1972, Pernod Ricard, Diageo from 2005, and since 2015 Proximo Spirits, which acquired Bushmills in a swap that sent Diageo full control of Don Julio tequila.
production: Bushmills mashes 100% unpeated malted barley and triple distils the spirit in relatively small copper pot stills, taking a narrow, clean cut — the source of the house's light, fruity style. The 10-year-old matures mainly in ex-bourbon American oak barrels, with a portion of Oloroso sherry casks contributing to the marriage before bottling at 40% ABV. All spirit is distilled, matured and bottled at the village site on Northern Ireland's Causeway Coast.
barrel: Mainly ex-bourbon American oak barrels with a portion of Oloroso sherry casks

tastingNotes.nose: Honey, vanilla, ripe apple and banana with light toasted oak.
tastingNotes.palate: Soft and silky — milk chocolate, malt, orchard fruit and a whisper of dried fruit sweetness.
tastingNotes.finish: Gentle and clean, honeyed malt fading with mild oak spice.

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature (18–20 °C) or with a small cube
facts: The famous 1608 licence was granted to the surrounding territory by King James I; the Bushmills company itself was registered in 1784.
Bushmills kept making malt whiskey through centuries when pot still blends dominated Irish distilling.
In 2015 Diageo traded Bushmills to Proximo Spirits in exchange for full ownership of Don Julio tequila plus cash.
The distillery sits a few miles from the Giant's Causeway on the Antrim coast.

timeline.event: King James I licenses distilling in the Bushmills territory
Old Bushmills Distillery Company registered
Fire destroys the distillery, which is rebuilt
Proximo Spirits acquires Bushmills from Diageo in the Don Julio swap

name: Yamazaki 12 Year
description: Yamazaki 12 is the whisky that introduced the world to Japanese single malt — launched in 1984 from Japan's first malt distillery and, in 2003, the first Japanese whisky to win gold at the International Spirits Challenge. Its style is precise and quietly exotic: ripe peach and persimmon, honey and coconut from American oak, sherried depth from Spanish oak, and the signature of Japanese mizunara oak — sandalwood and temple-incense spice found nowhere else in whisky. The global Japanese whisky boom turned it from bartender's secret to allocated trophy; official prices have climbed steeply and street prices sit far above even those. It remains the definitive expression of Suntory's elegant, blending-house approach to malt whisky, and the single bottle most drinkers name when Japanese whisky comes up.
history: Shinjiro Torii built the Yamazaki distillery in 1923 in a misty river-confluence valley between Osaka and Kyoto, hiring Masataka Taketsuru — later founder of rival Nikka — as his first distillery manager. For decades its malt vanished into blends; the Yamazaki single malt appeared only in 1984, as the flagship of Suntory's push to elevate the category. The 2003 ISC gold medal, the same era's international awards for its siblings, and the 2014-onward boom in Japanese whisky demand transformed the 12 into one of the world's most sought-after standard bottlings.
production: Yamazaki is engineered for variety: wooden and stainless washbacks, multiple yeast strains, and eight pairs of pot stills in different shapes and sizes produce dozens of distinct distillate styles on one site. The 12 marries malt matured in American white oak, Spanish oak sherry casks and rare Japanese mizunara oak, whose porous, slow-grown wood contributes incense and sandalwood notes. Water comes from the bamboo-covered slopes of the Yamazaki valley, and bottling is at 43% ABV.
barrel: American white oak, Spanish oak sherry casks and Japanese mizunara oak

tastingNotes.nose: Peach, persimmon and candied orange with honey, coconut and a thread of incense.
tastingNotes.palate: Silky and layered — ripe stone fruit, honeyed malt, cinnamon, and mizunara's sandalwood spice.
tastingNotes.finish: Long and gently spiced, dried fruit and incense with a clean oak fade.

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature (18–20 °C); in Japan also served mizuwari (with water and ice)

awards.award: Gold — International Spirits Challenge (first Japanese whisky to win ISC gold)

facts: Yamazaki is Japan's first malt whisky distillery, built in 1923 on a site prized for water once used by tea master Sen no Rikyu.
Masataka Taketsuru, who later founded rival Nikka, was Yamazaki's first distillery manager.
Mizunara oak is so porous and knotty that coopers consider it among the hardest woods to make casks from — and it takes centuries-old trees to yield staves.
Sustained shortages mean the 12 now sells well above its official price almost everywhere.

timeline.event: Construction of the Yamazaki distillery begins under Shinjiro Torii
Yamazaki 12 launched as Suntory's flagship single malt
First Japanese whisky awarded gold at the International Spirits Challenge
Official prices raised sharply amid continued global allocation
